_comment: Budgets sourced from CROSS_PLATFORM_LIGHTWEIGHT_CLIENT_AUTOPILOT.md §3.3 where an official number already exists (idle, active). The rest are proposed here for the first time, grounded in real measured baselines with margin -- per that same section's own rule: "Эти бюджеты являются стартовыми gates. Если платформа объективно требует иных значений, агент обязан приложить benchmark и обоснование." See AGENT_PERFORMANCE_BUDGETS.md for the measured baseline each was derived from.
_linux_rss_note: `linux_rss_mb_max` (run.sh only; run.ps1/Windows ignores it and uses `rss_mb_max`) exists because a real, independently-reproduced measurement found the Linux tray stack (GTK3+libayatana-appindicator) has a much heavier cold-start cost than the `rss_mb_max` figures assume: this dev session's own WSLg desktop measured ~30MB RSS for growth-layer-agent (comfortably under `rss_mb_max`), but a genuinely clean environment (fresh ubuntu:24.04 Docker container + Xvfb, no WSLg, no pre-warmed icon/font/GSettings caches) measured ~89MB idle / ~88MB active for the SAME binary -- while a plain `gtk3-demo` with no tray was only ~2MB RSS in the identical container, isolating the cost to the tray/appindicator stack's own icon-theme/D-Bus/font cold-start, not GTK itself or this agent's own logic. This is within the project's accepted up-to-2x tolerance for an official budget that turns out to be too strict for a real (if minimal) environment -- documented and budgeted for explicitly, not silently absorbed by a cushier dev environment. CI (ubuntu-latest, closer to this cold-start case than WSLg) uses `linux_rss_mb_max`; a genuine regression beyond this ceiling still fails the gate.

idle._source: CROSS_PLATFORM_LIGHTWEIGHT_CLIENT_AUTOPILOT.md §3.3 (2 MB/hour). Expressed as a per-hour rate, not a fixed per-window byte count, so it stays correct regardless of -DurationSeconds: run.ps1/run.sh measure real cumulative disk-write bytes over the scenario's own (short, ~14s) window and project that rate out to an hour -- an explicit, documented extrapolation from a short real sample, not a full hour actually measured (this benchmark's own scenario window is intentionally short; see TEST_REPORT.md's 'known gaps' for the same honest caveat on network, which this harness does not measure at all).

offline_queue._source: AG-PERF-001 proposal -- queue growth while backend unreachable must not cost more than idle's own RAM/CPU budget, since no upload work is happening, only local collection+enqueue (already covered by active's own budget) plus backoff retries (which must not busy-loop).

upload_burst._source: AG-PERF-001 proposal -- draining a large backlog is bursty CPU/network by design (that's the point of a burst); RAM should stay bounded since durable-queue streams one batch at a time, not the whole backlog into memory.

update_download._source: AG-PERF-001 proposal -- bandwidth-restrained by design (updater::DownloadConfig), so this budgets the DOWNLOADER's own overhead, not the artifact size itself. Real-measured baseline (updater/examples/update_bench.rs, a representative ~6MB artifact -- the real growth-layer-agent binary is ~2-3MB on both platforms, checked directly): 11.23MB RSS on Windows, 14.74MB on Linux. An earlier attempt used an arbitrary 20MB artifact and inflated RSS to 24-41MB by counting a large chunk of the artifact's own bytes rather than the downloader's overhead -- 30MB kept as the budget (comfortable margin over both real baselines) once the artifact size was made representative.

update_apply._source: AG-PERF-001 proposal -- staging is two atomic renames plus one file copy, should be near-instant and low-memory regardless of the binary's own size (streamed, not loaded whole into memory). Real-measured: staging+swap+commit of a real ~5MB fake binary completes before this harness's own first sample on both platforms (0 samples, exit success) -- too fast to produce a meaningful RSS/CPU number at this sampling granularity; verified functionally correct via update_bench's own real success/exit code instead.
